skill.name: gdpr-data-handling
skill.description: Implement GDPR-compliant data handling with consent management, data subject rights, and privacy by design. Use when building systems that process EU personal data, implementing privacy controls, or conducting GDPR compliance reviews.
skill.summary: Guide GDPR-aware consent, data subject rights, retention, and privacy-by-design implementation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-risk pattern hits, but review found no executable command runner, credential exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent. The detections are Markdown syntax, non-executable examples, database abstraction calls, GDPR terminology, and public reference links. Risk is low because the skill includes privacy implementation examples that still require legal and security review before production use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged backticks are Markdown code fences or inline file references, not Ruby shell execution. No evidence found of executable command invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown fences or inline resource paths. They do not define shell execution behavior.

low_findings.title: Network Detections Are Examples or Public References
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines contain async method examples, database abstraction calls, and links to GDPR guidance pages. No evidence found of hidden network calls or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only URLs are public GDPR resources, and the other cited lines are sample class methods. No secrets or user data are sent to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Data Flags Are Placeholder Data Model Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited lines discuss data categories and generic database abstractions. No evidence found of certificate files, private keys, SQLite files, or embedded credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations contain example database API calls and field names. They do not expose key material or local database files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Flags Are Context Terms
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited text uses GDPR phrases such as privacy by design, breach description, and notification workflow. No evidence found of weak algorithms, host reconnaissance, or port scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is narrative compliance guidance and data model examples. It contains no crypto implementation or reconnaissance command.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The combined code execution, network, and credential-access heuristic is not supported by semantic review. No prompt injection or malicious control text was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious categories collapse to Markdown syntax, examples, and reference links after review. I found no instruction to override analysis or perform unsafe actions.

content.value_statement: Teams processing EU personal data need clear consent, retention, and data subject request workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for GDPR-aware implementation reviews.
actual_capabilities: Explains GDPR lawful bases and data subject rights.
Provides consent management data model examples.
Outlines DSAR access, erasure, and portability workflows.
Suggests retention policy structures and enforcement steps.
Covers privacy by design and data minimization patterns.
limitations: Does not replace legal advice from qualified counsel.
Code examples are illustrative and need production hardening.
Does not verify current regulator guidance automatically.
Does not implement a complete compliance program by itself.

use_cases.title: Design Consent Workflows
use_cases.description: Plan consent capture, withdrawal, audit history, and purpose separation for a product feature.

use_cases.title: Review DSAR Handling
use_cases.description: Check access, erasure, rectification, and portability flows against practical GDPR response expectations.

use_cases.title: Plan Privacy Architecture
use_cases.description: Map minimization, retention, encryption, and breach notification controls before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Identify GDPR Requirements
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this product feature and identify likely GDPR data handling requirements. Focus on personal data, legal basis, consent, retention, and user rights.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Consent Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GDPR consent management design for this feature. Include consent categories, records to store, withdrawal handling, and audit evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate DSAR Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this data subject request workflow. Find gaps in verification, deadlines, access exports, erasure exceptions, and audit logging.

prompt_templates.title: Build Privacy Control Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this system architecture for GDPR privacy by design. Recommend controls for minimization, retention, encryption, breach response, vendor sharing, and documentation.

output_examples.input: Review a newsletter signup flow for GDPR consent.
output_examples.output: The response lists required consent choices, proof fields, withdrawal behavior, privacy notice updates, and checks for bundled consent.

output_examples.input: Plan a data subject access request process.
output_examples.output: The response outlines request intake, identity verification, source inventory, export preparation, exception handling, deadlines, and audit records.

output_examples.input: Assess retention for analytics and account data.
output_examples.output: The response separates data categories, assigns retention triggers, recommends anonymization for analytics, and flags legal review points.

best_practices: Confirm legal basis before designing processing workflows.
Separate examples from production code and add security review.
Document decisions, retention periods, and data subject request outcomes.
anti_patterns: Treating sample code as ready for regulated production systems.
Bundling unrelated consent purposes into one user choice.
Retaining personal data without a documented purpose and end date.

faq.question: Does this skill provide legal advice?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance that should be reviewed by qualified privacy counsel.

faq.question: Can it help with consent banners?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for consent categories, opt-in choices, proof records, and withdrawal handling.

faq.question: Can it design DSAR workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers request intake, verification, access exports, erasure handling, portability, deadlines, and audit trails.

faq.question: Does it execute code or call external services?
faq.answer: No. The skill is Markdown guidance with illustrative code examples and public reference links.

faq.question: Is it specific to EU personal data?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance focuses on GDPR duties for systems that process EU personal data.

faq.question: What should teams verify before production use?
faq.answer: Teams should verify legal basis, regulator guidance, data security controls, vendor obligations, and local implementation details.
